does: This paper introduces TrojanRAG, a backdoor attack method targeting large language models augmented with retrieval systems. The attack operates by constructing elaborate target contexts and trigger sets that are optimized through contrastive learning to create orthogonal backdoor shortcuts constrained to a parameter subspace. The method incorporates a knowledge graph to construct structured data enabling hard matching of target contexts at fine-grained levels, improving recall within the retrieval-augmented generation pipeline. The approach aims to achieve universal attack scenarios where a single trigger can manipulate LLM outputs across diverse contexts while maintaining robustness against model updates and iterations.
connects: This work builds on the emerging research on backdoor attacks against large language models, extending prior work that demonstrated LLMs are vulnerable to backdoor injection at various training stages. It responds to limitations in existing backdoor attacks on LLMs—namely their high computational cost, limited robustness, and degradation under model updates. The paper integrates retrieval-augmented generation systems as a novel attack surface, recognizing that RAG pipelines have become standard components in modern LLM deployments. It leverages contrastive learning techniques from metric learning and applies knowledge graph construction methods to enhance attack precision, combining these approaches with backdoor attack methodology.
enables: This research opens new directions for understanding vulnerabilities in RAG-augmented systems and their susceptibility to data poisoning attacks. It enables future work on defensive mechanisms specifically designed for retrieval-augmented generation, such as robust retrieval filtering and context verification methods. The work points toward evaluating the robustness of backdoors under continuous model updates and fine-tuning, which has implications for model security during deployment and iteration. It also suggests investigating whether similar joint attacks can exploit other modular components of LLM systems, and drives development of more sophisticated detection methods that monitor both retrieved content and model behavior simultaneously.
